name: Algonquin
language: Algonquin (Omàmiwininìmowin) — closely related to Ojibwe, Cree dialects
traditionalTerritories: Ottawa River valley and its tributaries, spanning eastern Ontario and western Quebec. Traditional Algonquin territory extends from Lake Témiscaming in the north to the Ottawa River corridor and Gatineau valley. The nation whose territory includes the site of Canada's capital city (Ottawa).
culturalNotes: The Algonquin are Anishinaabe people whose traditional territory spans the Ottawa River watershed. They are distinct from the broader Algonquian language family (which includes many nations). The Algonquins of Pikwakanagan are the only Ontario Algonquin First Nation with a reserve; other Algonquin communities are in Quebec. Algonquin people have been fighting for recognition of their unceded territory (which includes Ottawa/Gatineau) for generations. The term 'unceded Algonquin territory' is widely used in Canadian institutional acknowledgments. The Algonquin were traditionally hunters, fishers, and gatherers following the Ottawa River watershed system.
heritageSkillRelevance: Birchbark canoe construction — the Ottawa River was a major canoe highway; Algonquin canoe-building traditions are well-documented
Wiigiwaam (dome lodge) and longhouse construction using birchbark and cedar
Snowshoe construction for Ottawa valley terrain
Wild edibles of the Ottawa River valley — blueberries, fiddleheads, wild leeks (ramps)
Net fishing in Ottawa River tributaries
Maple sugar production — the Ottawa valley sugar bush tradition
communityAuthorizedResources: Algonquins of Pikwakanagan First Nation — algonquinsofpikwakanagan.com — Golden Lake, Ontario
culturalSovereigntyNotes: Material culture and traditional skill content (canoe building, maple syrup, wiigiwaam) is Level 1 with Algonquin attribution. Ceremonial protocols are Level 3-4 depending on type. Algonquin territorial acknowledgment language should always note the territory is unceded.
